summary: Hannes Mühleisen recounts the origin story of DuckDB, which grew out of research at CWI in the Netherlands. After engaging with R community data practitioners, he discovered they avoided databases entirely despite working with large datasets, due to slow client protocols and the burden of managing database servers. A prototype called MonoDB Lite proved the concept of in-process analytical databases but hit architectural limits, leading Hannes and Mark Raasveldt to build DuckDB from scratch—a multi-year effort that created an entirely new class of database system.
key_points: R community practitioners avoided databases entirely, preferring CSV files and hand-rolled data frame engines, because client-server setup was too burdensome
Database client protocols built in the 1980s were a major bottleneck—row-based, heavy serialization, never modernized
Running in-process is a harsher environment than client-server: you can't crash, can't modify global state like locale or working directory
The MonoDB Lite prototype validated product-market fit before DuckDB was built from scratch, reducing risk of the rewrite
Academic impact in data systems comes from building widely-used systems, not writing papers—citing Stonebraker's Turing Award as evidence
money_quotes: It turns out that they weren't using databases at all, right? They were storing their data in CSV files.
The client protocols of databases, built in the eighties, have never been touched, row-based, hardcore serialization stuff, not great.
Maybe we need to do something like SQLite, where there is actually no database server, but the database is in process. It's a library. But for analytics.
It turns out we invented a whole new class of database systems.
If you're running in-process, it's a much more harsh environment. You cannot just exit the process if things go wrong, because you're running in somebody else's process.
I decided that I'm going to start with Mark together to build a new database system, which is a monumental undertaking. It doesn't happen very often, and it's not successful 99% of the time.
My definition of success is not to write papers, it's to have impact.
We have a Turing Award in computer science for Mike Stonebraker, not because he writes amazing theoretical papers, but because he made systems like Ingress and Postgres.
key_insight: DuckDB was born from discovering that data practitioners refused to use databases due to client-server complexity, revealing an entirely missing category: the in-process analytical database.
